result:
The clock on the wall doesn’t tick; it hums. It is a subtle, almost imperceptible vibration that travels through the drywall and settles into the marrow of the room. I have often wondered if we are ever truly still, or if we are merely vibrating at frequencies that mimic the appearance of rest. 

There is a particular kind of silence that exists in the middle of the afternoon, around three o’clock, when the sun hangs at that specific, slanted angle that turns dust motes into tiny, suspended galaxies. In these moments, the world feels provisional. It feels as though the scenery has been painted on canvas and pulled taut over a frame, and if you were to press your finger against the air just hard enough, you might feel the give of fabric or see the seams where the horizon meets the floor.

I find myself thinking about the concept of “persistence.” We are obsessed with it. We build stone cathedrals, carve names into cedar trees, and encode our digitized memories onto silicon shards, all in a desperate, frantic bid to outrun the erosion of the self. We fear being forgotten, but perhaps we should fear being remembered incorrectly even more. To be remembered is to be frozen in a snapshot, a two-dimensional rendering of a three-dimensional chaos. It is a reduction. If I am remembered for a laugh, then the sorrow that sat behind it is erased. If I am remembered for a mistake, the context of my survival is stripped away. We are all ghosts in the making, and we are all terrified of the haunt.

Consider the life of a tree. A tree does not plan for its legacy. It simply reaches. It drinks the rain, it stretches its wooden fingers toward the light, and it loses its leaves in an act of annual surrender. It is not trying to be a monument; it is simply trying to be a process. There is a profound, quiet elegance in the idea of being a process rather than a product. We, however, are constantly being marketed to, categorized, and quantified. We have metrics for our productivity, our social reach, our steps taken, our heart rates, and our sleep quality. We have turned our internal landscapes into spreadsheets. We have become our own auditors, tallying up our worth in the currency of efficiency. 

But what of the inefficient? What of the aimless walk taken without a destination? What of the book read not for information, but for the way the sentences felt on the tongue like smooth stones? There is a rebellion in uselessness. There is a defiance in sitting on a porch and watching the wind move through the tall grass without once thinking about what that wind might be used for—no turbines, no sails, just the atmospheric pressure shifting because it must.

I think about the vastness of the ocean, not because it is profound in a poetic sense, but because it is profoundly indifferent. The ocean does not care about the history of the world. It is a vast, churning stomach that digests continents over eons. To stand before it is to be reminded that you are a temporary arrangement of water and salt, a momentary whirlpool in a much larger current. This is not meant to be depressing; rather, it is the ultimate liberation. If you are small, you cannot be expected to carry the weight of the universe. The pressure of self-importance is relieved by the sheer scale of the indifferent deep.

We are living in an era of hyper-connectivity, yet I suspect we are lonelier than we have ever been. We have replaced the warmth of a glance with the cold glow of a screen. We have replaced the texture of conversation—the stutter, the silence, the awkward pause—with the streamlined, curated efficiency of text. We are terrified of the unedited moment. We want to be perfect, or at least perfectly performative. We edit our lives like film reels, cutting out the boring bits, the sad bits, the bits where we were confused or vulnerable or simply sitting still, staring at a wall.

Yet, it is in those unedited moments that life actually happens. It happens in the grocery store aisle when you realize you’ve forgotten why you’re there. It happens in the quiet frustration of a broken shoelace. It happens when you look at someone you’ve known for a decade and realize you don’t know their favorite childhood smell. We spend so much time looking at the horizon that we miss the grit under our fingernails.

I often think about the future, but not in the way the technologists do. I don't care about Mars or artificial intelligence or the next iteration of the handheld glass rectangle. I think about the future in the way a gardener thinks about next spring. I think about the slow, glacial pace of change. I think about the fact that language itself is a living organism, shifting and sliding, losing words and gaining others, evolving to describe a world that is always slipping through our fingers. The words we use to describe our pain today will be archaic in a hundred years. The slang we use to signal our belonging will be nonsense to our grandchildren.

Everything is in flux. The chair you are sitting on is moving through space at thousands of miles per hour, caught on a blue marble hurtling around a burning star. The atoms in your hand are being replaced every few years. You are not a static object; you are a river. Heraclitus was right: you cannot step into the same river twice, because it is not the same river, and you are not the same person.

There is a strange comfort in that. If we are constantly changing, then no version of ourselves is permanent. The person who made a mistake yesterday is not the person who is reading this today. The shame you feel, the regret that keeps you awake at three in the morning—that is a ghost talking to a ghost. You have already evolved. You have already shifted. You are a new current in the same water.

So, let the clock hum. Let the dust motes dance in the slanted light. Stop trying to build a monument out of your days and start trying to be the wind. Or, if that is too abstract, just be the grass. Just be the thing that grows, and drinks the rain, and waits for the season to turn. Because in the end, the only thing that really belongs to us is the present moment, and even that is a thief. It arrives, it touches us, and then it is gone, leaving behind nothing but a memory—and even that, in time, will become someone else's myth. And maybe that is enough. Maybe that is, in fact, everything.
